mnemonics: PIMP My Proof: Use Premises, Implication rules, and a Conclusion to Prove validity.
keyPoints: Rules of Inference are valid argument forms used to construct logical proofs.
Modus Ponens (p → q, p, ∴ q) and Modus Tollens (p → q, ¬q, ∴ ¬p) are two fundamental rules.
A rule is valid if the conclusion is true whenever all the premises are true.
commonMistakes: Affirming the consequent: Assuming p is true because p → q and q are true.
Denying the antecedent: Assuming q is false because p → q and p is false.
Confusing the direction of implication; p → q is not equivalent to q → p.
